verses.text: Then came David to Nob to Ahimelech the priest: and Ahimelech was afraid at the meeting of David, and said unto him, Why art thou alone, and no man with thee? Guidance: David has slipped away from Jonathan and arrived at Nob, the priestly town a few miles north of Jerusalem, where the tabernacle has been relocated after Shiloh's destruction. The priest Ahimelech (great-grandson of Eli through Phinehas → Ahitub → Ahimelech) comes out trembling. The fear is not personal hostility but courtly intuition: David is the king's son-in-law and a national hero; he should not be arriving on foot, alone, unannounced. The unfamiliar sight is itself the question — 'why art thou alone, and no man with thee?' Ahimelech is asking the right question; David is about to give him the wrong answer. The chapter opens with a priest whose perceptiveness deserved a truthful reply.

And David said unto Ahimelech the priest, The king hath commanded me a business, and hath said unto me, Let no man know any thing of the business whereabout I send thee, and what I have commanded thee: and I have appointed my servants to such and such a place. Guidance: David lies. He invents a secret royal commission and a rendezvous with imaginary 'servants.' The motive is the same protective instinct that has run through chapters 19-20 — Saul's spies are everywhere, and David's true situation, if known, endangers everyone around him. The fabrication is meant to protect the priest from culpability — 'if you ever face Saul, you can honestly say you thought I was on the king's business.' But the lie sets up the disaster of chapter 22: Doeg, an eyewitness, will report only what he saw, and Ahimelech, unaware that he was helping a fugitive, will not be able to defend his innocence with the truth. David's pragmatic deception costs the priestly town its life. The episode is a warning about lies told for good reasons: they protect in the short term and bury the people you involved in the long term. Psalm 52, written about this very Doeg, shows David later wrestling with the consequences.

Now therefore what is under thine hand? give me five loaves of bread in mine hand, or what there is present. Guidance: David asks for the most basic provision — bread, five loaves, whatever is on hand. The verbal request is poignant in its specificity: 'in mine hand,' the lowliest practical detail. The future king, leaving on a wilderness journey, has not even loaves of his own. Faith is sometimes proved not in moments of giant-killing but in moments of asking strangers for bread. David has not yet learned that the LORD will provide; he is learning it by being hungry and asking. The wilderness years will teach him that what God supplies often arrives through unexpected hands — first a priest's, then a Moabite king's hospitality, then a foreigner widow's leftovers. The man who will later write 'the LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want' is right now wanting.

And the priest answered David, and said, There is no common bread under mine hand, but there is hallowed bread; if the young men have kept themselves at least from women. And David answered the priest, and said unto him, Of a truth women have been kept from us about these three days, since I came out, and the vessels of the young men are holy, and the bread is in a manner common, yea, though it were sanctified this day in the vessel. Guidance: Ahimelech has no ordinary bread on hand. The only bread available is the 'showbread' (lehem ha-panim, 'the bread of the Presence'), which by Mosaic law was the priests' food alone after it had served its week before the LORD (Lev 24:9). To give it to laymen was strictly speaking a violation. But Ahimelech makes a pastoral judgment: if the men are ritually pure (specifically, abstinent from sexual contact, which would render them temporarily unclean for sanctuary food per Lev 15:18), he will allow it. David affirms the purity: 'women have been kept from us about three days' — emergency travel, no sexual contact, ritual cleanness intact. Jesus will later cite this story (Matt 12:3-4, Mark 2:25-26) as proof that mercy can rightly override ceremonial precision in cases of human need. The principle is not that ritual is unimportant; the principle is that ritual exists to serve God and His people, not to starve them. Ahimelech reads the principle correctly under pressure.

And the priest answered David, and said, There is no common bread under mine hand, but there is hallowed bread; if the young men have kept themselves at least from women. So the priest gave him hallowed bread: for there was no bread there but the shewbread, that was taken from before the LORD, to put hot bread in the day when it was taken away. Guidance: 'So the priest gave him.' Ahimelech makes the call and acts. The narrator details the bread's nature: this was the very loaves just taken off the table that morning (the showbread was changed weekly on the Sabbath; we are probably reading a Sabbath afternoon), replaced by 'hot bread.' Ahimelech gives David the loaves that had been resting in God's presence for a week. There is something theologically beautiful in this gift: the bread that had borne the name and presence of the LORD becomes the rations of the LORD's anointed in exile. The hungry future king is fed by the symbol of God's continuous presence. Jesus will pick up this same logic when he tells the Pharisees that the one greater than the temple has come — and the one who is the true bread will feed the hungry himself. David's mouthful of showbread is a small foretaste of the gospel logic of grace meeting need.

Now a certain man of the servants of Saul was there that day, detained before the LORD; and his name was Doeg, an Edomite, the chiefest of the herdmen that belonged to Saul. Guidance: A single sinister verse, dropped without comment into the middle of the chapter. Doeg, an Edomite (foreigner, descended from Esau, Israel's old hereditary enemy) is at the sanctuary that day, 'detained before the LORD' — probably purifying himself from some ritual contamination, or perhaps fulfilling a vow. He is Saul's chief herdsman, a man whose loyalty lies with the throne, not with priests. The narrator names him but does nothing with him yet. The verse is a planted gun. In chapter 22 it will fire. The transaction David and Ahimelech think is private has a hostile witness. Sometimes the very moments of pastoral grace are also moments at which an observer is taking notes for a future betrayal. The chapter is teaching us that God's economy of provision and human economy of surveillance can run side by side, and the consequences of the second take longer to land but land harder.

And David said unto Ahimelech, And is there not here under thine hand spear or sword? for I have not brought my sword nor my weapons with me, because the king's business required haste. Behold, the sword of Goliath the Philistine, whom thou slewest in the valley of Elah, behold it is here wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod: if thou wilt take that, take it: for there is no other save that here. And David said, There is none like that; give it me. Guidance: David asks for a weapon. He is unarmed in fugitive country and needs something. Ahimelech tells him about the only sword in the sanctuary's keeping — Goliath's. The detail is one of the chapter's quiet wonders. David, after the valley of Elah, had brought Goliath's sword to the sanctuary as a votive trophy ('he put his armour in his tent,' 17:54, perhaps eventually delivered to the priests for safekeeping). The sword had hung wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod for years. Now David needs a weapon, and the only weapon available is the one God's deliverance had once placed in his hand. He says, 'There is none like that; give it me.' The sentence is doubly true: there is no other sword in the sanctuary, and there is no sword in the world like the trophy of the LORD's most famous deliverance. David walks out of Nob carrying the memory of God's power against giants. Sometimes God arranges that the equipment for today's fight is exactly the equipment from yesterday's victory, retrieved from the place you had laid it down in praise.

And David arose, and fled that day for fear of Saul, and went to Achish the king of Gath. Guidance: David flees that same day to Gath. Gath. Goliath's hometown. The Philistine city whose champion's sword David is now wearing on his hip. The choice is desperate; if there is one city in the world where David is most identifiable and least welcome, it is Gath. Why does he go there? Probably because no Israelite under Saul's reach is safe to harbor him, and Israel's enemies might count him as an enemy of their enemy. He gambles on being unrecognizable in plain Philistine clothing. The decision is the kind of decision panic produces — and the LORD will protect David through it, but not because David's strategy is sound. The chapter is showing David at his lowest tactical moment: fleeing into the territory of his most famous victims, carrying their champion's sword as identification. Faith and folly can sometimes share the same bus.

And the servants of Achish said unto him, Is not this David the king of the land? did they not sing one to another of him in dances, saying, Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands? And David laid up these words in his heart, and was sore afraid of Achish the king of Gath. Guidance: The disguise fails immediately. Achish's servants — perhaps including relatives of the men David has killed in his campaigns — recognize him at once. The phrase 'David the king of the land' is significant: Philistine intelligence has been tracking the David-Saul tension closely enough that they refer to David as Israel's de facto king. They quote the Israelite women's song back at him, which had presumably reached Gath through trade and spies. The recognition is total. 'David laid up these words in his heart, and was sore afraid.' He realizes his cover is destroyed and his death is moments away. The strongest king-in-waiting in Israel is reduced to terror in the very city of his greatest triumph. There is a special vulnerability when your reputation is what gets you cornered. David, in this moment, is going to find a way out that has nothing to do with strength or strategy and everything to do with humiliating improvisation.

And the servants of Achish said unto him, Is not this David the king of the land? did they not sing one to another of him in dances, saying, Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands? And David laid up these words in his heart, and was sore afraid of Achish the king of Gath. Guidance: The disguise fails immediately. Achish's servants — perhaps including relatives of the men David has killed in his campaigns — recognize him at once. The phrase 'David the king of the land' is significant: Philistine intelligence has been tracking the David-Saul tension closely enough that they refer to David as Israel's de facto king. They quote the Israelite women's song back at him, which had presumably reached Gath through trade and spies. The recognition is total. 'David laid up these words in his heart, and was sore afraid.' He realizes his cover is destroyed and his death is moments away. The strongest king-in-waiting in Israel is reduced to terror in the very city of his greatest triumph. There is a special vulnerability when your reputation is what gets you cornered. David, in this moment, is going to find a way out that has nothing to do with strength or strategy and everything to do with humiliating improvisation.

And he changed his behaviour before them, and feigned himself mad in their hands, and scrabbled on the doors of the gate, and let his spittle fall down upon his beard. Guidance: David acts. 'Feigned himself mad' — the Hebrew uses a denominative form of holel, the standard word for ecstatic-disorder behavior. He 'scrabbled on the doors of the gate' (the verb tavah is rare and uncertain; some translations say 'made marks like a child' on the gate's wooden doors), and 'let his spittle fall down upon his beard' (in Near-Eastern custom, allowing saliva to drool into one's beard was a sign of severe mental incapacity; the beard was the seat of male dignity and could not be voluntarily defiled by a sane man). The hero of the valley of Elah is now drooling on himself at the city gate, smearing the wood with a finger like a crazy person. Psalm 34 and Psalm 56, both inscribed to this very episode, will show us the inner life beneath the outward grotesque: David crying out to the LORD privately while looking pitiful publicly. Sometimes deliverance arrives through the abandonment of dignity. The man who refused Saul's armor because it didn't fit will, in this moment, put on whatever costume keeps him alive — including the costume of madness.

Then said Achish unto his servants, Lo, ye see the man is mad: wherefore then have ye brought him to me? Have I need of mad men, that ye have brought this fellow to play the mad man in my presence? shall this fellow come into my house? Guidance: Achish dismisses David with comedic exasperation. The pagan king's question — 'have I need of mad men?' — is the chapter's funniest line. He berates his own servants for wasting his time bringing him a man too disordered to be a useful prisoner or hostage. 'Shall this fellow come into my house?' — the rhetorical version of 'get him out of here.' David is released without a guard, without a trial, without even a beating. Ancient Near-Eastern cultures generally regarded the mentally ill as untouchable, partly out of religious fear (madness was thought to be divinely sent) and partly out of social embarrassment. David exploits the cultural taboo perfectly. The chapter closes with the LORD's anointed walking out of his enemies' city untouched, on his own feet, having played the part of a fool. Sometimes faithful survival looks ridiculous from the outside. David will write Psalm 34 about this very escape: 'I sought the LORD, and he heard me, and delivered me from all my fears… O taste and see that the LORD is good.' The taste of God's goodness sometimes comes after the taste of one's own spittle in one's own beard. Deliverance is rarely as dignified as the deliverers' songs make it sound.
